title: Weights & Biases
author: @weights_biases
text:
Skip to main contentPlatformModels >ExperimentsTrack and visualize your ML experimentsSweepsOptimize your hyperparametersTablesVisualize and explore your ML dataReportsVisualize and explore your ML dataTraining >Serverless RLFine-tune LLMs without managing GPUsARTOpen-source RL frameworkRulerAutomated reward function for RLInference >OpenAI OSSGPT OSS 20B, GPT OSS 120BAlibaba Qwen323B A22B, 23B5B Thinking, Coder 480BMeta LlamaLlama 4 Scout, 3.3 70B, 3.1 8BMoonshotAI KimiKimi K2Microsoft PhiPhi 4 Mini 3.8BHangzhou DeepSeekDeepSeek V3.1, V3-0324, R1-0528Z.aiZ.AI GLM 4.5Weave >TracesExplore and debug AI applicationsEvaluationsRigorous evaluations of AI applicationsPlaygroundExplore prompts and modelsAgentsObservability tools for agentic systemsGuardrailsBlock prompt attacks and harmful outputsMonitorsContinuously improve in productionCoreRegistryPublish and share your ML models and datasetsSkillsSkills for coding agentsSDKLog ML experiments and artifacts at scaleAutomationsTrigger workflows automaticallySolutionsUse CasesTrain LLMsFine-tune LLMsComputer VisionTime SeriesRecommender SystemsClassification & RegressionIndustriesAutonomous VehiclesCommunicationsFinancial ServicesHealthcare & Life SciencesPublic SectorScientific ResearchCase Studies >CanvaLearn how Canva leverages W&B to deploy modelsMicrosoftLearn how Microsoft uses W&B for their ML projectsToyotaLearn how Toyota uses W&B for autonomous drivingOpenAILearn how OpenAI Robotics uses W&B for large scale MLEnterpriseSecurityDeploymentPerformancePartnersSupportResourcesAI CoursesBlogArticlesPodcastWhitepapersEvents & WebinarsPressDocsPricingContactLog InSign UpW&B Fully Connected>Articles>RoboticsBuilding a production-grade MLOps pipeline for VLA modelsThis report covers the development of a compact vision-language-action model built around Gemma 3 270M. Brett YoungLast updated: June 11, 2026CommentVision-Language-Action (VLA) systems are moving beyond research demos and into workflows that look much closer to production ML infrastructure. Once a model maps images, language instructions, and robot state into actions, the surrounding pipeline starts to matter just as much as the model itself. Data provenance, experiment tracking, benchmark records, and deployment checks all affect whether a robotics system can be trusted, debugged, and improved.That matters because failures in robotics are costly. A bad text output can be ignored; a bad robot action can miss a grasp, waste a cycle, damage hardware, or create a safety issue. Reproducibility and observability therefore become core engineering requirements. Teams need to know which data mixture produced a checkpoint, which evaluation exposed a regression, and which model version is safe enough to promote.This article is built around a compact VLA project that replaces the original NVIDIA GR00T VLM, replacing it with Gemma-3 270M as the VLM backbone, while keeping SigLIP2 for vision and the pretrained GR00T diffusion action head for control. The full system comes in at about 1.5B parameters instead of roughly 3B, and it outperforms the base checkpoint on SimplerEnv, improving average success from 31.7% to 47.0% across six tasks.﻿What you will build in this tutorialThis tutorial builds a compact VLA pipeline for a language-conditioned robot task: given an image, an instruction, and a robot state, the model predicts the next discrete action. The example stays small enough to run and inspect, but the same pipeline structure applies to larger manipulation systems, industrial robots, and humanoid platforms.The workflow covers the full path from setup to model output:environment validationdataset creation with source metadatatraced model calls with Weavebaseline trainingexperiment logging in W&Bevaluation The article is organized in two parts. First, we walk through the concrete training and evaluation pipeline. Then, we look at the MLOps practices that made the project easier to reproduce, debug, compare, and improve.The dataset defines what the model is actually asked to learn. Tracing makes individual predictions inspectable instead of opaque. Training and W&B logging make model development repeatable rather than anecdotal. Evaluation decides whether a checkpoint is genuinely better, especially on the splits that matter for deployment. Registration and inference are what turn a training result into a versioned model that can be tested, compared, and used.Understand the VLA stack before you startThere are many ways to build a VLA model, but for this article, I will focus on one of the more common patterns currently in use: a vision encoder, a language backbone, and an action head. Images are encoded into visual features, the instruction is processed by the language model, and the combined representation is used to predict robot actions.That layout is useful because it breaks the system into parts that are easier to reason about. The vision encoder handles perception. The language model handles instruction conditioning and multimodal reasoning. The action head turns those representations into something the robot can execute. Different projects swap in different components, but the high-level structure is often similar.In this project, those roles are filled by SigLIP2, Gemma-3 270M, and a pretrained GR00T diffusion action head. The exact combination matters less here than the pipeline around it: once a VLA stack has multiple components, multiple training stages, and downstream benchmark results, you need a way to track what changed, what improved, and what is safe to keep using.﻿The vision tower is SigLIP2, which encodes the input image into visual tokens. The visual tokens pass through a learned two-layer MLP connector that projects them into Gemma's embedding space. This connector enables the pretrained vision encoder and language model to communicate despite being trained separately. During the earliest stage of training, this connector is the only trainable component, so its role is to learn a clean mapping from SigLIP features into a form that Gemma can interpret.Once projected, the visual tokens are concatenated with the tokenized language instruction and processed by Gemma-3 270M. In this setup, Gemma serves as the policy's multimodal reasoning core. It integrates the visual scene with the textual task description and produces hidden states that capture the current observation in instruction-conditioned form. Those hidden states are then passed into the action stack.The action module is inherited from GR00T. More specifically, the policy uses the pretrained GR00T DiT action head (about 1B parameters) from nvidia/GR00T-N1.6-fractal. Because Gemma's hidden size is 1152 while the GR00T action head expects 2048-dimensional inputs, an additional learned projection layer is inserted between the language model and the action head. This bridge is necessary to connect the smaller language backbone to the existing GR00T control stack without changing the action head architecture itself.The DiT action head is trained using a flow-matching objective. Instead of directly predicting the final action chunk in a single step, the model is trained to transform noise into a valid action sequence conditioned on the observation and instruction. During training, the model is shown intermediate points along a path between random noise and a ground-truth action chunk, and it learns the direction that moves each intermediate point toward the clean action target. In other words, the DiT learns a conditional vector field over action trajectories. At inference time, the policy starts from noise and follows the learned flow to generate an action chunk consistent with the current scene and task.The role of VLMs in VLAsVLMs often serve as the perception-and-language backbone inside a VLA system. Their job is to turn raw images and task instructions into representations that are useful for control. In a robotics setting, that means helping the system identify what is present in the scene, which parts of the image are relevant to the instruction, and how the language refers to objects, locations, and task intent.A compact VLA pipeline usually begins with visual encoding and language encoding as separate steps. Camera observations are passed through a vision backbone to produce visual features that capture objects, scene layout, and spatial context. The instruction is passed through a text backbone to produce language features that represent intent, constraints, and references such as color, object type, or destination. Robot state is encoded alongside them, so the policy has access to information such as gripper status, joint values, or end-effector pose. Those streams are then fused and passed into an action head that predicts the next control output.In practical terms, the VLM provides the part of the system that grounds the instruction in the current observation. It helps the policy determine what the command refers to, where the relevant object is, and which visual details matter for the task at the current timestep. The action head takes that grounded representation and turns it into behavior.This separation matters because robot data is usually limited and messy. Teleoperation logs are expensive to collect, labels are imperfect, and real-world scenes vary in ways that are hard to cover fully. A pretrained VLM can carry strong visual and language priors before any robot-specific training begins. That gives the control layer a much better starting point than learning perception, language grounding, and action prediction from scratch on a small robotics dataset.Repository structure and tutorial flowBefore getting into the actual training steps, it helps to understand how the repository is organized and why the tutorial moves between directories.At a high level, this project (which you can clone here) is split across three main areas:Eagle/Eagle2_5/ for the Gemma3-270M vision-language backbone workIsaac-GR00T/ for robot dataset preparation, GR00T fine-tuning, and evaluationmlx_gr00t/ for export and validation utilities for running the trained model on Apple Silicon with MLXThe full training path is a 4-stage pipeline. The first two stages happen in the Eagle codebase and prepare the multimodal backbone. The last two happen in Isaac-GR00T and fine-tune the robot action model.4-stage pipelineTrain the MLP connector with the LLM frozen (Eagle/Eagle2_5/train_stage1_gemma3.sh)Full VLM fine-tune with the vision backbone frozen (Eagle/Eagle2_5/train_stage2_gemma3_270m.sh)Balanced GR00T training across 8 datasets (Isaac-GR00T/train_balanced_from_270m.sh)Fractal-only post-training with top LLM layers unfrozen (Isaac-GR00T/post_train_fractal.sh)Directory layoutThe repo structure implied by the tutorial looks like this: gemma_robot/ ├── Eagle/Eagle2_5/ │ └── shell/ (train_stage1/2 scripts) │ └── shell/download_pretraining_data.sh ├── Isaac-GR00T/ │ ├── download_all_datasets.sh │ ├── train_balanced_from_270m.sh │ ├── post_train_fractal.sh │ ├── eval_compare.sh │ └── eval_watcher.sh That structure matters because each directory has a different role in the workflow.The Eagle/Eagle2_5/ directory is where the Gemma-based visual-language backbone is prepared. This is where the SigLIP encoder, the Gemma language model, and the multimodal connector are aligned before GR00T training begins.The Isaac-GR00T/ directory is where the robot-specific part of the work happens. This includes downloading datasets, running balanced multi-dataset VLA training, post-training on fractal, and evaluating the resulting checkpoint against the stock NVIDIA model.The mlx_gr00t/ directory is the deployment-style side of the project. It contains the export and validation utilities needed to run the trained model on Apple Silicon with MLX.Now that the directory structure is clear, the tutorial can be read as a sequence of concrete scripts run from specific locations.Step 1: Set up the machine and authenticateBefore running any training, the environment needs to be ready. That means a GPU machine, authenticated access to Hugging Face and W&B, the Isaac-GR00T training dependencies, and the Eagle2.5 code available on PYTHONPATH when GR00T scripts are launched.Typical setup actions include:huggingface-cli loginwandb loginInside Isaac-GR00T/, install the training dependencies:pip install -e ".[train]"This is the prerequisite stage that makes the rest of the tutorial runnable.Step 2: Download the backbone assets and pretraining dataThe first real step happens in Eagle/Eagle2_5/. This is where the Gemma-based visual-language backbone is prepared before GR00T fine-tuning begins.Run:cd Eagle/Eagle2_5/bash shell/download_pretraining_data.shThis script downloads the SigLIP vision encoder weights, the Stage 1 and Stage 2 Eagle pretraining datasets. It is idempotent, so rerunning it should skip work that is already complete.Step 3: Train the Stage 1 Eagle VLM connectorStill inside Eagle/Eagle2_5/, the first model-training step is Stage 1.Run:bash shell/train_stage1_gemma3.shThis stage keeps the Gemma language model frozen and keeps the SigLIP vision backbone frozen. Only the MLP connector is trained. Conceptually, this is the first alignment step between the image encoder and the language model.The main output of this step is the first Eagle checkpoint, which becomes the initialization point for Stage 2.Here are the logs for my run: ﻿Run set﻿Step 4: Fine-tune the Stage 2 Eagle VLM backboneOnce Stage 1 finishes, the next step is the fuller VLM fine-tune.Run:bash shell/train_stage2_gemma3_270m.shThis stage starts from the Stage 1 checkpoint. The vision backbone stays frozen, but the language model and the MLP connector are now trainable. The resulting checkpoint is the actual Gemma3-270M backbone that GR00T training uses later.This is the handoff point between the Eagle side of the project and the GR00T side. Here are the logs for my run: ﻿Run set﻿Step 5: Download the full robot dataset suiteOnce the smoke test passes, the next step is to pull the full training corpus.Run:bash download_all_datasets.shThis downloads the full multi-dataset suite used by GR00T training, including fractal, Bridge, LIBERO variants, DROID, RoboCasa, and the optional NVIDIA Panda tasks. The script is designed to be resumable and idempotent, which matters because the full pull is large and may take hours.At this point, the robot data layer is in place, and the tutorial can move into actual GR00T fine-tuning.Step 7: Run balanced GR00T trainingThe first GR00T training stage is the balanced multi-dataset run.Run:bash train_balanced_from_270m.shThis script checks that the datasets are present, finds the Gemma3-270M backbone checkpoint, and launches the main balanced training phase. In this stage, all core datasets are mixed together, with fractal kept at roughly half the training distribution. This is the broad multi-embodiment learning stage of the tutorial.Here are the logs for my run: ﻿Run set﻿Step 8: Run fractal post-trainingAfter balanced training, the model is specialized back toward the target Google-robot domain with fractal-only post-training.Run:bash post_train_fractal.shYou can also point it at a specific checkpoint:bash post_train_fractal.sh --checkpoint /path/to/checkpointThis step lowers the learning rate, switches to fractal-only, and unfreezes only the top language model layers while continuing to tune the projector and diffusion head. It also starts eval_watcher.sh in the background so evaluation can happen while training progresses.This is the final training stage of the tutorial.﻿Run set﻿﻿I also logged videos of the intermediate evaluations as the training run progressed:﻿﻿Run set﻿Step 9: Evaluate against stock GR00T-N1.6Once post-training produces candidate checkpoints, the next step is to compare them directly against the stock NVIDIA baseline.Run:bash eval_compare.shOr evaluate a specific checkpoint:bash eval_compare.sh --ours /path/to/checkpointYou can also scale the rollout settings:N_EPISODES=50 N_ENVS=5 bash eval_compare.shThis script runs six SimplerEnv Google-robot tasks and compares the stock model against the newly trained model under the same protocol. It logs task-by-task success rates, summary metrics, and evaluation artifacts into W&B and Weave. This is where the tutorial moves from training to evidence.Here are the results: ﻿Run set﻿﻿﻿Run set﻿At this point, we’ve walked through the tutorial itself: how the repository is laid out, which scripts drive each stage, and how the full training path moves from backbone preparation to GR00T fine-tuning, and finally evaluation.But getting a pipeline to run is only part of the story. What makes a project like this useful over time is the operational layer underneath it: the practices that make training repeatable, data trustworthy, experiments interpretable, and model comparisons credible. That is the MLOps side of the project.Now we will look at the MLOps concepts that made the tutorial practical. Each one matters on its own, but they also reinforce each other. Reproducibility makes reruns possible. Validation protects the inputs. Observability makes behavior visible. Lineage connects outputs back to their causes. Evaluation turns metrics into decisions. Continuous improvement becomes possible only when all of those pieces are already in place.So with the mechanics of the training pipeline covered, we can now dive into the bigger MLOps ideas underneath it, and show how each one appeared in this project in a concrete, non-theoretical way.ReproducibilityReproducibility is one of the most basic ideas in MLOps, but in practice, it is also one of the most frequently lost. In a machine learning project, a result is never just the output of a model definition. It depends on a chain of decisions: which data was downloaded, which preprocessing assumptions were applied, which checkpoint was used as the starting point, which script launched the run, which hyperparameters were active, and which evaluation path was used afterward. If those pieces are not recorded in a stable, executable form, then a result can exist without being truly recoverable.That matters in any ML project, but it matters even more in a VLA system. A robotics policy can change behavior due to small differences in instruction formatting, dataset composition, normalization statistics, or data preprocessing. Those changes may not look dramatic in training logs, but they can produce very real differences in task success. If the run cannot be recreated, it becomes difficult to separate meaningful improvements from setup accidents.In this project, reproducibility began with a simple habit: shell scripts were treated as the source of truth for the workflow's major actions. There was a script for backbone pretraining data download, a script for Stage 1 Eagle training, a script for Stage 2 Eagle training, a script for smoke-test data download, a script for full dataset download, a script for balanced GR00T training, a script for fractal post-training, and a script for evaluation. That meant the path from raw setup to final checkpoint was not hidden in terminal history or scattered across ad hoc notes. It was visible in the repository as a series of named entry points. W&B reinforced that reproducibility layer by logging the run configuration alongside the Git commit hash, which is especially helpful when returning to an experiment later and trying to answer the deceptively simple question of which version of the code actually produced a given result. In practice, that kind of metadata often matters just as much as the training hyperparameters themselves.The broader lesson is that reproducibility in ML is not only about seeds and config files. It is also about having a clean operational path that lets another person, or your future self, understand what actually happened. In this project, reproducibility came from treating scripts, stage boundaries, and checkpoint flow as first-class parts of the system rather than as afterthoughts around the model code.ObservabilityObservability is what allows a team to see what the system is doing instead of guessing. In MLOps terms, that usually means metrics, artifacts, evaluation outputs, run histories, and comparison views that make model behavior inspectable across time. Without observability, a machine learning workflow is mostly opaque. Runs succeed or fail, losses go up or down, and checkpoints accumulate, but it becomes hard to answer the more important questions: what changed, why it changed, and whether it actually helped.For VLA models, observability has to go beyond standard loss graphs. Training loss matters, but it is not the whole story. A robotics policy can appear reasonable on scalar metrics and still fail on key tasks. It can also improve on average while regressing on a specific behavior that matters more than the average. Because the model is learning to map multimodal context to actions, observability must include task-level evaluation and artifact-level inspection, not just a dashboard of training curves.In this project, observability came primarily through W&B, with Weave extending the evaluation side. The balanced training phase and fractal post-training phase both logged runs in the same W&B project, making it possible to view training curves, run configurations, Git commits, saved checkpoints, and evaluation summaries in a shared space. That alone is already valuable, because it means the project did not rely on loose collections of screenshots or manually copied notes.Once a VLA policy leaves the training loop and starts operating in a more production-like setting, scalar metrics are no longer enough. A success rate can tell you that something changed, but it cannot show you what the robot actually did. In practice, that missing context matters a lot. A failed rollout might reflect hesitation, a bad approach trajectory, a grasping error, or unstable control near the end of the episode. All of those count as failures, but they point to very different problems.That is why video is worth treating as part of the observability layer. In this project, W&B handled the structured experiment record - metrics, runs, and comparison tables - while Weave was useful for tracing evaluation-time rollouts and attaching the resulting videos to those calls. That keeps qualitative behavior close to the rest of the evaluation record instead of leaving clips scattered across local directories.At a high level, the pattern is simple: run a rollout with the VLA policy, save the episode video, and return that clip from a traced Weave function.import weavefrom moviepy.editor import VideoFileClip﻿weave.init("gemma-gr00t-vla")﻿@weave.opdef model_rollout(model, env, instruction: str): # Pseudocode: # 1. reset the environment # 2. run the VLA policy on image + instruction + robot state # 3. save the rollout video to disk﻿ video_path = "videos/google_robot_pick_object_ep04.mp4"﻿ clip = VideoFileClip(video_path, has_mask=False, audio=True) return clip.subclip(0, 5)﻿model_rollout( model=vla_policy, env=google_robot_env, instruction="pick up the object",)Weave provides a nice dashboard for inspecting rollout traces and viewing the videos attached to them. In this setup, that means the rollout clip is stored with the traced call rather than being left as a separate file, which makes it easier to review behavior directly in the same interface. That is the main reason I found it useful here: it adds a qualitative layer to the evaluation without requiring a separate workflow to manage rollout videos.﻿﻿﻿﻿Observability also extended into the training process itself through intermediate evaluation. During training, the system periodically logged evaluation rollouts, including videos of the robot attempting tasks and the corresponding success rates for those intermediate checkpoints. That made it possible to watch behavior evolve over the course of training, rather than treating evaluation as something that only happens at the very end. This is especially important in robotics and VLA work because scalar loss alone does not indicate whether the model is actually improving at the behaviors you care about. Sometimes the most informative signal is not a number but a short clip showing how the policy is failing, hesitating, or beginning to generalize correctly.Observability is especially useful in the connection between training and evaluation. The project ran head-to-head comparisons against the baseline model and logged the task-level results back into W&B. After training, the evaluation outputs for both the baseline model and the fine-tuned model were written into W&B Tables side by side, making it easy to compare task success, aggregate metrics, and qualitative rollout artifacts in one place. That allowed training behavior and downstream task performance to live in the same operational view. Instead of saying “the loss looked good” and separately “evaluation seemed okay,” the system could show actual checkpoint-by-checkpoint evidence during training, followed by final side-by-side comparisons once training was complete.Observability also played a role in model sizing decisions. A larger, roughly 1B gemma variant was compared against the 270M setup on an early slice of VLA data, and the difference did not justify the extra scale. That kind of decision is exactly why observability matters. It prevents architecture choices from being driven by intuition alone. When runs are logged cleanly and compared within a common system, a surprising result becomes something the team can trust rather than something it argues about.In a project like this, even basic metric logging goes a long way. The balanced GR00T training run, the fractal post-training run, and any ablation or model-size comparison can all be viewed in the same system with their configs attached. That makes it much easier to answer practical questions such as whether a lower loss came from a better model, a different dataset mixture, or just a different learning-rate schedule. The code for logging metrics with W&B is minimal:import osimport wandb﻿run = wandb.init( project=os.getenv("WANDB_PROJECT", "gemma-gr00t-vla"), name="balanced-270m-train", config={ "stage": "balanced_train", "vlm": "gemma-3-270m", "vision_encoder": "siglip2", "action_head": "GR00T diffusion head", "datasets": ["fractal", "bridge", "droid", "robocasa", "libero"], "batch_size": 64, "lr": 1e-4, },)﻿for step in range(1, 1001): # pseudocode loss = 1.0 / (step ** 0.5) lr = 1e-4﻿ wandb.log( { "train/loss": loss, "train/lr": lr, "train/global_step": step, }, step=step, )﻿run.finish()The deeper point is that observability reduces ambiguity. In complex ML systems, ambiguity is expensive. It wastes training cycles, makes regressions harder to diagnose, and encourages overconfidence in weak signals. This project benefited from observability not just because it produced cleaner reports, but because it made the actual state of the system visible enough to support decisions.This example is also simplified, but it shows the core pattern: log the run configuration, record metrics consistently, and make the training stage explicit in the run metadata.LineageLineage is the idea that every model artifact should have a recoverable history. A checkpoint should not be an orphaned file with a name and a timestamp. It should be connected to the code, data, preprocessing assumptions, hyperparameters, and evaluation outputs that produced it. Lineage is what makes a model understandable as a product of a process rather than as a standalone object.﻿This is often discussed in abstract terms, but in practice, it becomes important the moment a project has multiple training phases, multiple checkpoints, and multiple plausible explanations for why performance changed. That is exactly the situation in a VLA workflow. If a new model behaves differently, is it because the data mixture changed? Because the post-training phase used a lower learning rate? Because different layers were unfrozen? Because a modality file changed? Because a normalization bug was fixed? Without good lineage, those questions become difficult to answer reliably.This project built lineage through a combination of stage-based scripts, tracked runs, and external artifact storage. The stage structure itself created a natural chain of lineage:Stage 1 produced a connector-trained VLM checkpoint,Stage 2 produced the usable Gemma-based backbone,Stage 3 produced balanced GR00T checkpoints, andStage 4 produced the fractal-specialized checkpoints. Because each of those phases had its own script and its own run context, it was possible to interpret a final checkpoint in terms of the path that led to it.W&B reinforced that chain by attaching metrics, configs, and artifacts to the run records. The external model-hosting step reinforced this further by placing checkpoints in a durable storage layer rather than leaving them as local files on a single machine. That is important both operationally and conceptually. Operationally, durable artifact storage protects against machine loss and makes checkpoints easier to share or resume from. Conceptually, it treats model files as managed outputs of a pipeline rather than as temporary byproducts.More generally, this project really showed how important dataset curation is for training VLAs. A big part of the job is deciding which data the model should learn from in the first place. The choice of datasets, the balance between them, and the decision to do additional post-training on more targeted data can all meaningfully change what the model ends up good at. As those choices become more numerous, it becomes increasingly important to track exactly which data mixture went into each stage and each run.EvaluationEvaluation was the part of the workflow that answered the question training alone could not: did the fine-tuned model actually get better at the tasks we cared about? New checkpoints, lower loss, and longer training runs can all look like progress, but for a VLA policy, the real test is always behavior. The only meaningful way to judge that was to compare the post-trained model directly against the baseline on the target task suite.That mattered especially in this project because the goal was not just to improve metrics in the abstract, but to improve performance in the Google-robot fractal setting. Keeping evaluation tied to those target tasks helped prevent the project from confusing general training progress with actual improvement in the deployment domain. A checkpoint only counted as better if it behaved better where it mattered.One of the stronger MLOps aspects of the project was that evaluation was not left as a final manual step. It was effectively automated through background processes that could monitor for new checkpoints, launch evaluation runs, recover from interruptions, and log results back into the experiment-tracking system. That made the whole process much more repeatable and much less dependent on someone remembering to run comparisons by hand at the end.That automation also changed the role evaluation played in the project. Instead of only checking performance after all training was finished, it became possible to observe how performance changed over the course of post-training and to see whether newer checkpoints were actually better than earlier ones. That is important because more training is not always better training, especially when specializing a model to a narrower domain.From an MLOps perspective, this is where evaluation and promotion come together. A checkpoint should not be treated as the new best model just because it is the latest artifact produced by the pipeline. It should earn that status through direct comparison against the baseline. In this project, evaluation served as a bridge between experimentation and decision-making: training produced candidate models, and evaluation determined whether any of them deserved to replace the starting point.﻿W&B Tables were especially useful because they made evaluation results easy to inspect at the episode level rather than only at the aggregate level. During evaluation, outputs from the baseline and post-trained policies were logged in a single table view, including success, rollout length, and the recorded video for each model. That made it easy to compare the two policies side by side and quickly see whether the fine-tuned model was actually improving behavior or just changing aggregate metrics in a less interpretable way. Here's an example of how to log tables with W&B: import osimport wandb﻿run = wandb.init(project="gemma-gr00t-vla", job_type="evaluation")﻿episode_table = wandb.Table( columns=[ "task", "episode", "stock_success", "stock_frames", "stock_video", "ours_success", "ours_frames", "ours_video", ])﻿def evaluate_checkpoint(model, env, task, num_episodes): results = []﻿ for episode_idx in range(num_episodes): obs = env.reset(task=task, seed=episode_idx) done = False frames = 0﻿ video_path = f"videos/{model.name}_{task}_ep{episode_idx}.mp4" os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(video_path), exist_ok=True)﻿ # Pseudocode: env handles recording internally and writes to video_path. env.start_recording(video_path)﻿ while not done: action = model.act(obs) obs, reward, done, info = env.step(action) frames += 1﻿ env.stop_recording()﻿ results.append( { "task": task, "episode": episode_idx, "success": int(info.get("success", False)), "frames": frames, "video_path": video_path, } )﻿ return results﻿task = "google_robot_close_drawer"num_episodes = 10﻿stock_results = evaluate_checkpoint( model=stock_model, env=google_robot_env, task=task, num_episodes=num_episodes,)﻿ours_results = evaluate_checkpoint( model=posttrained_model, env=google_robot_env, task=task, num_episodes=num_episodes,)﻿for stock_ep, ours_ep in zip(stock_results, ours_results): episode_table.add_data( stock_ep["task"], stock_ep["episode"], stock_ep["success"], stock_ep["frames"], wandb.Video(stock_ep["video_path"]), ours_ep["success"], ours_ep["frames"], wandb.Video(ours_ep["video_path"]), )﻿wandb.log({"episode_table": episode_table})run.finish()As with the earlier snippets, this is a simplified example meant to show the logging pattern. In a real evaluation script, the model wrapper, environment reset, action normalization, recording behavior, and success criteria would come from the evaluation stack.Alternative use cases for the same VLA pipelineOne of the more interesting takeaways from this project is that the pipeline itself is much more general than the specific Google-robot fractal setup it was tested on. The exact model weights, datasets, and evaluation tasks would change from one application to another, but the overall workflow - data selection, staged training, experiment tracking, checkpoint evaluation, and promotion - can carry over to a wide range of embodied AI problems.Some alternative use cases include:Warehouse picking, where a robot must identify, grasp, and place a variety of objects under changing lighting, clutter, and shelf conditions.Visual inspection, where the model helps a robot or camera-equipped platform detect defects, anomalies, or assembly errors in industrial environments.Mobile manipulation, where a robot must navigate through space and then perform a manipulation task at the destination.Humanoid instruction following, where a more general embodied agent takes language commands and convert them into long-horizon physical behavior.What changes across these use cases is not just the model configuration but the full operating context: the physical environment, the robot embodiment, the task objectives, and the data collected from those settings. A warehouse system is trained on very different observations and behaviors than a visual inspection platform or a humanoid instruction-following robot. The scenes, objects, dynamics, sensors, action spaces, time horizons, and failure modes all shift, so the dataset must change as well. In practice, transferring the pipeline to a new use case usually requires redefining the data schema, recollecting or curating task-relevant demonstrations, adjusting the action representation, and updating the evaluation suite to reflect the new environment and operational risks.What stays the same is the operational structure around the model. No matter which embodiment or task family is being targeted, you still need reliable tracking of runs and artifacts, clear lineage between checkpoints and the stages that produced them, repeatable evaluation against the right target-task suite, and some kind of registry or promotion workflow so that “latest trained model” does not automatically become “model we trust.” Those are the pieces that make the pipeline reusable instead of one-off.Conclusion This project showed that MLOps becomes useful very quickly once VLA training starts, involving multiple stages, multiple datasets, repeated post-training, and many candidate checkpoints. At that point, it is no longer enough to just run training jobs and save model files. It becomes necessary to keep track of what was trained, which data went into each stage, how checkpoints relate to one another, and whether newer models actually improved on the baseline.In this project, experiment tracking kept runs organized and comparable. Lineage made it possible to trace checkpoints back through the training process and the datasets behind them. Evaluation made model quality something that had to be demonstrated on the target tasks rather than assumed from loss curves or training completion. Promotion decisions could then be based on evidence instead of recency.The project also made clear how much the final behavior depends on the choice of dataset and the specialization strategy. The selection of training datasets, the balance between them, and the decision to perform further post-training on a narrower target domain all directly affected what the policy became good at. That made disciplined tracking and evaluation especially important, because performance changes could come from data decisions just as easily as from model or optimization changes.More generally, the same operational structure can carry over well beyond this specific benchmark and robot setup. Warehouse picking, visual inspection, mobile manipulation, humanoid instruction following, service robotics, and edge-deployed industrial systems would all bring different action spaces, sensor stacks, sequence horizons, deployment constraints, and safety requirements. Even with those differences, the underlying needs remain familiar: track experiments clearly, preserve lineage across training stages, evaluate against the right target tasks, and promote only checkpoints that have earned trust. That is the part of the pipeline that remains useful across embodiments, domains, and deployment settings.In other words, the compact VLA model is the technical result, but the larger lesson is operational: reliable embodied AI depends on knowing exactly how a model was trained, what evidence supports it, and why it should be trusted before it is promoted.﻿﻿﻿Add a commentTags: Articles, Community Posts, Robotics, W&B
